subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim and relevance are clearly stated and linked to strategies for objective processing
The theory of confirmation bias is described and connected explicitly to the planned replication
Independent and dependent variables are operationalized clearly in the hypothesis
weaknesses: Hypothesis lacks a directional prediction, reducing precision
Some conceptual overlap between deductive reasoning and confirmation bias undermines theoretical clarity
The theoretical framework could be deepened with models such as dual-process or heuristic-analytic theories
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction satisfies the top band descriptors by stating and explaining the aim, accurately describing the model, and operationalizing variables. However, minor conceptual conflations and the absence of a directional hypothesis prevent it from reaching the full 5â6 level. A band-2 deduction is therefore not warranted, but one mark is removed to reflect these omissions, yielding a score of 4.

strengths: Convenience sampling and participant characteristics are described
Several controlled variables (seating distance, standardized instructions) are identified and linked to bias reduction
weaknesses: Research design type (e.g., repeated-measures) is neither named nor justified
Discussion of sampling representativeness (e.g., demographic balance) is missing
Rationale for materials and some controlled variables (e.g., lighting, card design) is incomplete
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While sampling and control measures are documented, the absence of an explicit, justified research design places this work in the 1â2 band. Although other strands approach the 3â4 level, the fundamental design explanation gap limits this criterion to 2 marks.

strengths: McNemarâs test is an appropriate inferential choice for paired nominal data
The bar chart addresses the hypothesis by comparing mean correct responses across rounds
Findings interpretation links statistical results to the research hypothesis
weaknesses: Confusion between p-value and test statistic terminology affects accuracy
Graph axis labels and caption lack clarity on units and sample size
Inconsistent percentage notation and absence of effect size reporting reduce interpretive depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis applies both descriptive and inferential statistics appropriately and links results to the hypothesis, aligning with the 3â4 band. Multiple technical errors and omissions prevent full accuracy, justifying a mid-band score of 3.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to Wasonâs original study and confirmation-bias theory
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained, and directly tied to validity and reliability
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified to enhance data quality
weaknesses: Conclusions generalize to everyday contexts without acknowledging sample limits
Proposed modifications do not address sampling strategy improvements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates strong linkage of findings to theory, thorough explanation of strengths and limitations, and well-justified modifications, fitting the 5â6 band. Minor overgeneralization and a missing sampling modification prevent top-band depth, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart is supplied and directly compares mean correct responses across rounds, addressing the hypothesis. Axis labelling (decimal vs percentage) is imprecise, constituting an error. Thus the work fits the middle band with score 3.

The student links the p-value to accepting the research hypothesis and notes how descriptive results support that conclusion, connecting findings to hypothesis. Nonetheless, wording such as âno hay suficiente evidencia para descartar nuestra hipÃ³tesis de investigaciÃ³nâ shows conceptual confusion, so interpretation accuracy is partial. Hence 3 fits the rubric.

Both IV (card arrangement across two rounds) and DV (written accuracy scored as 1/0 and later expressed as percentage correct) are not only identified but also operationalised; the hypothesis and null hypothesis are stated. Some wording is awkward, yet the operational detail meets the 5-6 descriptor.

Convenience sampling is named; age and subject-knowledge criteria are justified as ways to reduce bias, giving a brief rationale for participant choice. That explanation moves the strand into the 3-4 band, though the justification is limited, so 3 is awarded.

Confirmation bias and Wasonâs card-selection task are accurately described, and the narrative makes an explicit connection between the cognitive mechanism and the planned replication. Minor omissions (e.g., fuller coverage of dual-process theory) keep the work short of the very top of the band, but the required description and link are present and accurate.

Findings are compared in detail with Wasonâs original study and theoretical expectations around confirmation bias, discussing similarities and potential developmental differences. Depth is good but not exhaustive; 5 represents solid discussion within the top band.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate how confirmation bias influences decision-making â and then explicitly explains why this is relevant (identifying bias could lead to strategies for more objective processing). The explanation, while concise, goes beyond a mere statement, fulfilling the descriptor for the top band, though the depth is not exceptional, so 5 rather than 6 is appropriate.

Several strengths (replication fidelity, clear instructions) and limitations (age group, recording confusions, comprehension issues) are not only stated but explained in terms of impact on validity/reliability, meeting the 5-6 descriptor. Breadth is reasonable but lacks the depth for 6, so 5 is appropriate.

Materials and several controlled variables (distance between desks, absence of prior briefing, identical instructions) are listed and in some cases linked to prevention of communication or demand effects, demonstrating partial explanation. Coverage is uneven, but enough rationale is given for the lower half of the 3-4 band.

Proposed modifications (comprehension checks, clearer response format, balanced gender sample) are explicitly tied to stated limitations and justified as means to improve data quality and reduce bias, satisfying the criteria for the highest band; again, depth is good but not exceptional, so 5 is awarded.

The student describes that two rounds of the Wason task will be shown and that all participants complete the same worksheet, but there is no explicit identification or explanation of the design type (e.g., repeated-measures) or its suitability. Description only places the work in the 1-2 band; detail is sufficient for the upper end of that band.

Descriptive statistics and an appropriate inferential test (McNemar) are used for paired nominal data. However, percentage notation is confused (0.56%) and the verbal statement of the decision criterion contains conceptual errors, indicating inaccuracies. This matches âappropriate statistics applied but contain errorsâ (3-4 band); 3 is given.

annotations.comment: The aim is clearly stated and its relevance explained, linking identification of biases to strategies for objective information processing.
Expand the theoretical framework by integrating dual-process or heuristic-analytic models to deepen the conceptual underpinning of confirmation bias.
There's confusion between test statistic and p-value (referring to Z < 0.05 instead of p < 0.05); correct terminology to improve accuracy.
Standardize presentation of percentages and decimals (e.g., 0.56 vs 56%) for clarity and consistency in descriptive statistics.
The description conflates deductive reasoning and confirmation bias; differentiate the cognitive processes to avoid conceptual overlap and strengthen theoretical clarity.
The hypothesis states an effect but lacks a directional prediction (e.g., greater selection of even-numbered cards); include this for precision.
Clarify the logic of card selection and the falsification principle in Wasonâs task to link procedure and theory more explicitly.
Seating distance and standardized instructions are controlled to reduce communication biasâa clear strength in experimental control.
Suggest adding a balanced sampling modification (e.g., equal gender representation) to enhance generalizability and reduce selection bias.
The conclusion generalizes to everyday decision contexts without acknowledging limits of adolescent sample; temper claims accordingly.
The study design is described but the type (e.g., repeated-measures) isnât named or justified; specify and explain its suitability for this task.
Independent and dependent variables are operationalized clearly, with card arrangement and percentage correct responses defined successfully.
Sampling description notes convenience technique but omits discussion of demographic representativeness (e.g., gender); address this limitation.
Provide rationale for each material choice (e.g., why use a chronometer rather than self-timing) to strengthen methodological justification.
The choice of McNemarâs test is appropriate for paired nominal data, reflecting correct alignment of test to data type.
Interpretation states âno hay suficiente evidencia para descartarâ yet concludes support for the hypothesis; rephrase to correctly reflect p<0.05 logic.
Did the student consider practical significance (e.g., effect size) or focus solely on statistical significance?
Could selection bias from a convenience sample affect internal validity? Consider alternative sampling to mitigate this risk.
Is the link between identifying biases and developing objective processing strategies explicitly detailed? Consider specifying potential strategies.
Report degrees of freedom and the chi-square statistic for the McNemar test to provide full inferential detail.
Axis labels in Graph 1 arenât clearly marked as percentages or proportions; adjust labels to reflect units accurately.
Consider reporting an effect size or measure of association to contextualize the practical impact beyond statistical significance.
The evaluation discussion aligns findings with Wasonâs original study and theoretical expectations, demonstrating strong linkage to background theory.
Strengths and limitations are linked to methodological factors impact reliability, showing clear relevance to research objectives.
Proposed modifications focus on comprehension checks but donât address improving the sample strategy; link sampling changes to limitations.
Suggest future research directions such as varying age groups or cultural backgrounds to test the robustness of confirmation bias effects.
Connecting empirical results to potential applications in critical-thinking training demonstrates strong evaluation of real-world relevance.
Controlled variables include seating and instructions, but factors like lighting or card design arenât mentioned; consider adding these controls.
Graph caption lacks clarity on units and sample size per bar; specify number of participants and units in the caption.
Unify language register in statistical descriptions; avoid mixing Spanish and English to maintain consistency.
